summary: Joel responds to Jakob Nielsen's article on offshore usability by broadening the argument to all offshore software design. He argues that software teams fail when design, management, and programming are physically separated. Drawing from personal experience with managers spread across New York, Singapore, and Hyderabad, he contends that effective teams depend on hundreds of small daily interactions that remote separation makes impossible.
key_points: Offshore usability problems are a specific case of the broader offshore design problem
Software teams fail when design or management are physically separated from programming by large distances and time zones
Effective project teams rely on hundreds of small daily interactions that cannot happen across time zones
There is a double standard where companies readily offshore developers but would never offshore management
money_quotes: Software teams are not successful when design or management are done in a different physical location than programming.
A good project team relies on hundreds of small interactions a day.
What I don't understand is people who think it's OK to move the developers ten time zones away from their managers and expect good results.
Those same people would scream bloody murder if you told them that you were going to send the whole management team to Bangalore or Beijing.
key_insight: Effective software development depends on hundreds of small daily interactions between designers, managers, and programmers, making large time-zone separations between these roles a recipe for failure.
